title: Data Retrieval and Performance
breadcrumbs: Development Considerations for Performance and Scalability
Performance Guidelines for Component Logic and Implementation
text: Data Retrieval and Performance When retrieving data into components, it is useful to understand the performance impact of various approaches to data retrieval. read Trigger The read trigger is referenced each time an I/O operation is performed, so the number of ProcScript instructions in the read trigger should be kept to a minimum. If a read trigger contains read as the only statement, the action is performed entirely by 3GL. If there are other statements in the READ trigger, the ProcScript interpreter is also involved, which is always slower that 3GL. read The read ProcScript statement fetches an occurrence from the hitlist. If necessary, it first builds the next step of the hitlist. The read statement should be used only in the read trigger. It is recommended that you avoid using the read/lock statement (which results in paranoid locking) for online transactions because so many locks are required. SQL Statements For some databases, the way SQL statements are formulated determines how efficiently the statement is processed. In such situations, it might be worthwhile to access the RDBMS directly using SQL statements rather than have Uniface generate and pass these statements. It is possible to invoke stored procedures using the activate statement. Depending on the system load and the RDBMS, having the RDBMS process statements contain an order by clause can also improve performance. However, bypassing Uniface and the constraints checked by Uniface, can cause data consistency and integrity to be violated. The need to include many SQL statements in an application usually indicates that the application model is not optimal. In this case, consider reviewing the design. Using SQL also reduces the application's portability. where and u_where Some databases are sensitive to the way in which SQL statements are formulated, which can affect performance. If you are using such a database, pay special attention to how Uniface passes SQL statements to the RDBMS. Uniface includes the u_where clause in the SQL statement, following pre-defined rules, whenever it needs to access the database. However, you might need to use the where clause to ensure that SQL statements are optimally formulated for the RDBMS. Making the Last Occurrence Current If the $ENABLE_CACHE_ALWAYS assignment setting is present, each occurrence is retrieved into the select cache when moving to the last occurrence, for example, when using the statement: setocc "*", -1 If the $ENABLE_CACHE_ALWAYS assignment setting is not present, the select cache is not used when moving to the last occurrence. This improves performance when information concerning other records is not required. If the last occurrence needs to be retrieved, it is recommended that you use the statement: setocc "*", -1 rather than the statement: setocc "entity", -1 When the asterisk (*) form of setocc instruction is used, Uniface disables the select cache mechanism, resulting in better performance. An alternative is to define a command button that saves the profile, clears the form, resets the profile, and then retrieves the occurrences in reverse order by dynamically setting the u_where clause in the read trigger. Related Links $ENABLE_CACHE_ALWAYS Allow the select cache mechanism when moving to the last occurrence. read Set the access mode. u_where Provide a DBMS-independent profile for selection with read and selectdb. setocc Make a specific occurrence the current occurrence.
